<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanical Gripper Kinematics & Force Analysis

### 2.1.1 Slider-Crank Gripper ŌĆö Velocity Ratio

The velocity ratio (mechanical advantage) of a slider-crank gripper is determined by the **instantaneous geometry** of the linkage. Let:
- $r$ = crank length
- $l$ = connecting rod length
- $\theta$ = crank angle (measured from the slider's axis)
- $\phi$ = connecting rod angle

Position of the slider:
$$x = r\cos\theta + l\cos\phi$$

By the loop-closure equation:
$$r\sin\theta = l\sin\phi$$

Differentiating with respect to time:
$$r\cos\theta \cdot \dot{\theta} = l\cos\phi \cdot \dot{\phi}$$

The slider velocity is:
$$\dot{x} = -r\sin\theta \cdot \dot{\theta} - l\sin\phi \cdot \dot{\phi}$$

The **velocity ratio** $VR$ is the ratio of slider velocity to crank (input) angular velocity:

$$VR = \frac{\dot{x}}{\dot{\theta}} = -r\sin\theta - l\sin\phi \cdot \frac{\dot{\phi}}{\dot{\theta}}$$

From the differentiated loop-closure equation:
$$\frac{\dot{\phi}}{\dot{\theta}} = \frac{r\cos\theta}{l\cos\phi}$$

So:
$$\boxed{VR = -r\sin\theta - l\sin\phi \cdot \frac{r\cos\theta}{l\cos\phi} = -r\left(\sin\theta + \frac{\sin\phi \cos\theta}{\cos\phi}\right) = -\frac{r\sin(\theta + \phi)}{\cos\phi}}$$

### 2.1.2 Force Amplification

The **mechanical advantage** (inverse velocity ratio) gives the force amplification:
$$MA = \frac{1}{|VR|} = \frac{\cos\phi}{r\sin(\theta + \phi)}$$

The gripping force on the jaw:
$$\boxed{F_{grip} = T_{motor} \cdot \frac{MA}{r_{finger}} = T_{motor} \cdot \frac{\cos\phi}{r \cdot r_{finger} \cdot \sin(\theta + \phi)}}$$

Where $r_{finger}$ is the moment arm from finger pivot to contact point.

### 2.1.3 Toggle Linkage (Wrist Pinch)

Toggle mechanism provides high mechanical advantage near the **toggle (locked) position**:

$$F_{out} = \frac{F_{in}}{2\tan\alpha}$$

Where $\alpha$ is the toggle link angle from the line of action. As $\alpha \to 0$, $F_{out} \to \infty$ (theoretically). In practice, $\alpha_{min} = 3┬░$ŌĆō$5┬░$ to avoid jamming.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Toggle linkages are widely used in the da Vinci surgical robot's EndoWrist instruments** for the needle driver ŌĆö they provide the high clamping force needed to grip a curved suture needle without motor stall.

---

## 2.2 Cam Gripper ŌĆö Kinematic Equations

### 2.2.1 Cam Profile Equations

For a **disk cam with radial follower**, the displacement $s$ as a function of cam rotation $\theta$ is specified by the **motion program**:

| Cam Motion Law | Displacement $s(\theta)$ | Application |
|----------------|--------------------------|-------------|
| **Constant velocity** | $s = \frac{h \cdot \theta}{\beta}$ (linear rise) | Low-speed, simple grippers |
| **Simple harmonic** | $s = \frac{h}{2}\left(1 - \cos\frac{\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)$ | Smooth motion, low vibration |
| **Cycloidal** | $s = h\left(\frac{\theta}{\beta} - \frac{1}{2\pi}\sin\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)$ | No jerk, high-speed |
| **3-4-5 Polynomial** | $s = 10h\left(\frac{\theta}{\beta}\right)^3 - 15h\left(\frac{\theta}{\beta}\right)^4 + 6h\left(\frac{\theta}{\beta}\right)^5$ | Smoother than cycloidal |

Where:
- $h$ = total lift (cam rise)
- $\beta$ = rise angle (cam angle during rise portion)
- $\theta$ = current cam angle (in rise phase)

### 2.2.2 Pressure Angle and Force Transmission

The **pressure angle** $\alpha$ is the angle between the force direction on the follower and the direction of follower motion. For a radial translating follower:
$$\tan\alpha = \frac{\dot{s}}{r_0 + s} \cdot \frac{ds/d\theta}{1}$$

For a knife-edge follower (simplest case):
$$\tan\alpha = \frac{ds/d\theta - e}{r_0 + s}$$

Where $e$ = eccentricity (offset).

The force on the cam from the follower:
$$F_{cam} = F_{follower} \cdot \cos\alpha$$

The torque required to drive the cam:
$$T_{cam} = F_{cam} \cdot L_{eff} = F_{follower} \cdot L_{eff} \cdot \cos\alpha \cdot \mu_{cam}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** If $\alpha > 30┬░$, the cam may **self-lock** or **jam**. Always check maximum pressure angle during cam design.

---

## 2.3 Magnetic Gripper ŌĆö Comprehensive Force Analysis

### 2.3.1 Maxwell Stress Tensor

The attractive force on a pole face is derived from the **Maxwell stress** in the air gap:

$$F_{mag} = \frac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}$$

For an **electromagnet** with $N$ turns and current $I$, the field in the gap is:
$$B = \frac{\mu_0 N I}{l_g}$$

(where $l_g$ = air gap length), so:
$$\boxed{F_{mag} = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 I^2 A}{2 l_g^2}}$$

### 2.3.2 Magnetomotive Force (MMF) and Reluctance

Using the magnetic circuit analogy (Ohm's law for magnetic circuits):
$$\mathcal{F} = \Phi \cdot \mathcal{R}$$

Where:
- $\mathcal{F} = NI$ (MMF, in ampere-turns)
- $\Phi$ = magnetic flux (Wb)
- $\mathcal{R} = \frac{l}{\mu A}$ (reluctance)

For a simple magnetic circuit with air gap:
$$F_{mag} = \frac{(NI)^2 \mu_0 A}{2 l_g^2}$$

### 2.3.3 Energy Method

Energy stored in the air gap:
$$W = \frac{B^2}{2\mu_0} \cdot A \cdot l_g = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 I^2 A}{2 l_g}$$

Force from virtual work:
$$F = -\frac{\partial W}{\partial l_g}\bigg|_{I=\text{const}} = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 I^2 A}{2 l_g^2}$$

Confirms the previous result.

### 2.3.4 Permanent Magnet Grippers

For a permanent magnet with residual flux density $B_r$:
$$F_{PM} \approx \frac{B_r^2 A}{4\mu_0}$$

Typical values:
- NdFeB (N52): $B_r \approx 1.4$ T ŌåÆ $F/A \approx 390$ kPa
- Ferrite: $B_r \approx 0.4$ T ŌåÆ $F/A \approx 64$ kPa
- AlNiCo: $B_r \approx 1.0$ T ŌåÆ $F/A \approx 200$ kPa

---

## 2.4 Vacuum Gripper ŌĆö Comprehensive Theory

### 2.4.1 Theoretical Holding Force

The theoretical maximum force on a perfectly sealed vacuum cup is:
$$F_{ideal} = (P_{atm} - P_{vac}) \cdot A$$

For **complete vacuum** ($P_{vac} = 0$):
$$F_{ideal} = P_{atm} \cdot A = 101325 \cdot A \text{ (N, with A in m┬▓)}$$

### 2.4.2 Real-World Force with Sealing Factor

$$F_{real} = (P_{atm} - P_{vac}) \cdot A \cdot \eta_{seal}$$

$\eta_{seal}$ accounts for:
- Surface roughness (0.95 for polished metal, 0.6 for rough concrete)
- Air leakage
- Cup deformation
- Workpiece porosity

### 2.4.3 Venturi Vacuum Generator

A typical vacuum generator uses the **Venturi effect**:

$$P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2$$

Compressed air at high speed through a constriction creates low pressure at the suction port.

Vacuum level achieved: typically **ŌłÆ80 to ŌłÆ90 kPa** (gauge), i.e., absolute pressure **10ŌĆō20 kPa**.

### 2.4.4 Cup Deflection and Lifespan

The vacuum cup compresses under load. The **contact stress distribution** follows Hertzian contact theory for soft materials:

$$\sigma_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{6 F E^{*2}}{R^2 \pi^3}}$$

Where $E^*$ is the effective modulus and $R$ is the cup radius.

---

## 2.5 Pneumatic Gripper ŌĆö Comprehensive Theory

### 2.5.1 Cylinder Force

The force generated by a pneumatic cylinder:
$$F_{cyl} = P \cdot A_{piston} - F_{friction} - F_{backpressure}$$

For double-acting cylinder with rod (extend stroke):
$$F_{extend} = P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} - P_{back} \cdot \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$$

For retract stroke:
$$F_{retract} = P \cdot \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4} - P_{back} \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

Where:
- $D$ = piston diameter
- $d$ = rod diameter
- $P$ = supply pressure
- $P_{back}$ = back pressure on opposite side

### 2.5.2 Air Consumption

Air consumption per cycle (for double-acting):
$$V_{cycle} = \frac{\pi}{4}\left[D^2 L + (D^2 - d^2)L\right] \cdot \frac{P_{supply} + P_{atm}}{P_{atm}}$$

Convert to standard liters per minute (SLPM) based on cycle rate.

### 2.5.3 Response Time

The time to fully actuate (90% stroke) is approximated by:
$$t_{90\%} \approx \frac{V_{cylinder}}{C_v \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P / P_{supply}}} \cdot 3$$

Where $C_v$ is the valve flow coefficient.

### 2.5.4 Gripping Force for Pneumatic Gripper

For a parallel-jaw pneumatic gripper with toggle linkage:
$$F_{grip} = F_{cyl} \cdot \frac{L_{cylinder}}{L_{finger}} \cdot \frac{1}{2\tan\alpha}$$

Where $L_{cylinder}$ and $L_{finger}$ are effective lever arms.

---

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Slider-Crank Gripper Force Transmission

### Problem Statement
A slider-crank gripper has crank length $r = 25$ mm, connecting rod $l = 75$ mm, and a finger link of length $L_f = 50$ mm pivoted at a distance $a = 30$ mm from the slider. The motor torque is $T = 2.5$ N┬Ęm. Find the gripping force on the jaw when the crank angle is $\theta = 60┬░$ from the slider axis. Take efficiency $\eta = 0.9$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Loop Closure Equation**

The geometry of the slider-crank mechanism gives:
$$r \sin\theta = l \sin\phi$$

Substitute the values:
$$25 \sin 60┬░ = 75 \sin\phi$$
$$25 \times 0.8660 = 75 \sin\phi$$
$$21.65 = 75 \sin\phi$$
$$\sin\phi = 0.2887$$
$$\phi = \arcsin(0.2887) = 16.78┬░$$

**Step 2: Velocity Ratio Calculation**

Using the derived formula:
$$VR = -\frac{r \sin(\theta + \phi)}{\cos\phi}$$

Compute $\theta + \phi$:
$$\theta + \phi = 60┬░ + 16.78┬░ = 76.78┬░$$

Compute $\sin(76.78┬░)$ and $\cos(16.78┬░)$:
$$\sin(76.78┬░) = 0.9734$$
$$\cos(16.78┬░) = 0.9574$$

So:
$$VR = -\frac{25 \times 0.9734}{0.9574} = -\frac{24.335}{0.9574} = -25.42 \text{ mm} = -0.02542 \text{ m}$$

The negative sign indicates the slider moves opposite to the assumed direction as the crank rotates.

**Step 3: Slider Velocity from Motor Speed**

Assume motor angular speed $\omega = 100$ rad/s (typical small servo):
$$\dot{x} = VR \cdot \omega = 0.02542 \times 100 = 2.542 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4: Force Amplification**

Mechanical advantage:
$$MA = \frac{1}{|VR|} = \frac{1}{0.02542} = 39.34$$

**Step 5: Slider Force**

Power balance (input power = output power ├Ś efficiency):
$$F_{slider} \cdot |\dot{x}| = T \cdot \omega \cdot \eta$$
$$F_{slider} = \frac{T \cdot \omega \cdot \eta}{|\dot{x}|} = \frac{2.5 \times 100 \times 0.9}{2.542} = \frac{225}{2.542} = 88.5 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6: Force at Slider Using Mechanical Advantage**

Alternatively:
$$F_{slider} = T \cdot \frac{MA}{r} = 2.5 \times \frac{39.34}{0.025} = 3934 \text{ N}$$

**Step 7: Moment Balance at Finger Pivot**

The slider pushes the finger at distance $a = 30$ mm from pivot. The jaw contact is at distance $L_f = 50$ mm from pivot.

Moment equilibrium at pivot:
$$F_{slider} \cdot a = F_{grip} \cdot L_f$$
$$F_{grip} = F_{slider} \cdot \frac{a}{L_f} = 88.5 \times \frac{30}{50} = 53.1 \text{ N}$$

**Step 8: Final Result with Efficiency Verification**

$$F_{grip} = 53.1 \text{ N (per jaw)}$$

For two jaws gripping symmetrically, total holding capability = $2 \times F_{grip} = 106.2$ N.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** Slider-crank grippers typically provide 50ŌĆō200 N gripping force. Our result (53.1 N) is on the lower end because of the geometry ŌĆö increasing $a/L_f$ ratio or reducing $\theta$ would increase force.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Toggle Linkage Force Amplification

### Problem Statement
A toggle linkage gripper has two links of length $L = 40$ mm each, with the input force applied at the midpoint. The toggle angle (between each link and the line of action of output force) is $\alpha = 5┬░$. The input force is $F_{in} = 50$ N. Find the output clamping force.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Free Body Diagram of Toggle Joint**

At the toggle joint, two links meet at angle $2\alpha$. The input force $F_{in}$ acts along the symmetry axis.

**Step 2: Force Resolution**

Each link carries a compressive force $F_c$. By symmetry, this force has:
- A component along the line of action: $F_c \cos\alpha$ (each side)
- A perpendicular component: $F_c \sin\alpha$ (cancel by symmetry)

Total resistance:
$$F_{out} = 2 F_c \cos\alpha$$

**Step 3: Force Balance at Joint**

The input force must balance the component of link force opposing motion:
$$F_{in} = 2 F_c \sin\alpha$$

**Step 4: Solving for Link Force**

$$F_c = \frac{F_{in}}{2\sin\alpha}$$

**Step 5: Output Force**

$$F_{out} = 2 F_c \cos\alpha = 2 \cdot \frac{F_{in}}{2\sin\alpha} \cdot \cos\alpha = F_{in} \cdot \frac{\cos\alpha}{\sin\alpha} = \frac{F_{in}}{\tan\alpha}$$

$$\boxed{F_{out} = \frac{F_{in}}{\tan\alpha}}$$

**Step 6: Numerical Evaluation**

For $\alpha = 5┬░$:
$$\tan(5┬░) = 0.0875$$
$$F_{out} = \frac{50}{0.0875} = 571.4 \text{ N}$$

**Step 7: Mechanical Advantage**

$$MA = \frac{F_{out}}{F_{in}} = \frac{1}{\tan\alpha} = \frac{1}{0.0875} = 11.43$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** As $\alpha \to 0┬░$ (full toggle), $MA \to \infty$. But practically, $\alpha_{min} = 3┬░$ to avoid binding/jamming. At $\alpha = 3┬░$, $MA = 19.08$, giving $F_{out} = 954$ N ŌĆö more than sufficient for needle clamping in surgery.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Magnetic Gripper Holding Force

### Problem Statement
An electromagnetic gripper has a U-shaped iron core with 500 turns of wire, a cross-sectional area of $A = 4 \text{ cm}^2 = 4 \times 10^{-4}$ m┬▓, and an air gap of $l_g = 2$ mm = $2 \times 10^{-3}$ m. The current is $I = 2$ A. Find the holding force on a flat steel plate.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Field in the Air Gap**

The magnetic field intensity in the air gap (assuming iron has very high permeability, so MMF drop is only in the gap):
$$H_{gap} = \frac{NI}{l_g} = \frac{500 \times 2}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1000}{0.002} = 5 \times 10^5 \text{ A/m}$$

**Step 2: Flux Density in the Gap**

$$B = \mu_0 H = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5 \times 10^5 = 0.6283 \text{ T}$$

**Step 3: Maxwell Stress Calculation**

Force on the pole face:
$$F = \frac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0} = \frac{(0.6283)^2 \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}{2 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7}}$$

Compute numerator:
$$B^2 A = 0.3948 \times 4 \times 10^{-4} = 1.579 \times 10^{-4}$$

Compute denominator:
$$2\mu_0 = 2 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} = 2.513 \times 10^{-6}$$

So:
$$F = \frac{1.579 \times 10^{-4}}{2.513 \times 10^{-6}} = 62.85 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Verification Using Direct Formula**

$$F = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 I^2 A}{2 l_g^2} = \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 500^2 \times 2^2 \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}{2 \times (2 \times 10^{-3})^2}$$

Numerator:
$$4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 2.5 \times 10^5 \times 4 \times 4 \times 10^{-4} = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 400 = 5.027 \times 10^{-4}$$

Denominator:
$$2 \times 4 \times 10^{-6} = 8 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$F = \frac{5.027 \times 10^{-4}}{8 \times 10^{-6}} = 62.83 \text{ N} \checkmark$$

**Step 5: Pressure on Pole Face**

$$p = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{62.83}{4 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.57 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 0.157 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** This gripper generates ~62.8 N holding force, sufficient for grasping a small steel medical instrument (e.g., 50 g needle driver, requiring ~0.5 N to hold against gravity ŌĆö so 100├Ś safety factor).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Vacuum Gripper Holding Force

### Problem Statement
A vacuum gripper with a circular silicone suction cup of diameter $D = 50$ mm is used to lift a glass slide in a cleanroom. The vacuum pump achieves $P_{vac} = 30$ kPa absolute (i.e., ŌłÆ71.3 kPa gauge). Sealing efficiency is $\eta_{seal} = 0.85$. Find the maximum holding force.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Pressure Differential**

$$\Delta P = P_{atm} - P_{vac} = 101.325 - 30 = 71.325 \text{ kPa} = 71325 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2: Effective Sealing Area**

For a circular cup of diameter $D = 50$ mm = 0.05 m:
$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4} = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Ideal Holding Force**

$$F_{ideal} = \Delta P \cdot A = 71325 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} = 140.1 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Real Holding Force with Sealing Factor**

$$F_{real} = F_{ideal} \cdot \eta_{seal} = 140.1 \times 0.85 = 119.1 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Safe Working Load (with Safety Factor 4)**

$$F_{safe} = \frac{F_{real}}{4} = \frac{119.1}{4} = 29.8 \text{ N} \approx 3 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 6: Verify with Shear Force Consideration**

If the gripper holds the slide vertically, shear stress acts on the cup. The shear strength of silicone:
$$\tau_{silicone} \approx 5 \text{ MPa} = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

Required shear area:
$$A_{shear} = \frac{F_{hold}}{\tau} = \frac{29.8}{5 \times 10^6} = 5.96 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 = 5.96 \text{ mm}^2$$

This is well within the cup geometry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** A 50 mm diameter vacuum cup can safely hold a 3 kg glass slide in a cleanroom, with 4├Ś safety factor.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Pneumatic Gripper Cylinder Sizing

### Problem Statement
A double-acting pneumatic parallel-jaw gripper must apply $F_{grip} = 80$ N per jaw. The linkage has mechanical advantage $MA = 1.5$. Air supply pressure is $P = 0.6$ MPa. The piston rod has diameter $d = 0.3D$ (where $D$ is the piston diameter). Find the required piston diameter.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Required Cylinder Force**

For the gripper, the cylinder force must overcome the linkage and provide the grip force:
$$F_{cyl} = \frac{F_{grip}}{MA} = \frac{80}{1.5} = 53.33 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Cylinder Force Equation (Extend Stroke)**

$$F_{extend} = P \cdot A_{piston} - P_{back} \cdot A_{rod} = F_{cyl}$$

For simplicity, assume $P_{back} \approx 0$ (exhaust to atmosphere):
$$P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = F_{cyl}$$

**Step 3: Solve for D**

$$D^2 = \frac{4 F_{cyl}}{P \pi} = \frac{4 \times 53.33}{0.6 \times 10^6 \times \pi} = \frac{213.3}{1.885 \times 10^6} = 1.132 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

$$D = \sqrt{1.132 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.01064 \text{ m} = 10.64 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Select Standard Size**

Use the next standard size: $D = 12$ mm. With this size:
$$F_{actual} = 0.6 \times 10^6 \times \frac{\pi (0.012)^2}{4} = 67.86 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Retract Stroke Force**

$$A_{rod} = \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.012^2 - 0.0036^2)}{4} = 1.017 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

$$F_{retract} = P \cdot A_{rod} = 0.6 \times 10^6 \times 1.017 \times 10^{-4} = 61.0 \text{ N}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** A 12 mm bore, double-acting pneumatic cylinder with 3.6 mm rod can deliver 53 N, providing 80 N grip force per jaw with 1.5├Ś linkage MA. Both extend and retract strokes are sufficient.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Gripper Types

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this matrix for KTU 2-mark comparison questions.**

| Criterion | Mechanical | Magnetic | Vacuum | Pneumatic |
|-----------|-----------|----------|--------|-----------|
| **Force range** | 10ŌĆō500 N | 5ŌĆō200 N | 1ŌĆō500 N | 20ŌĆō2000 N |
| **Stroke range** | 5ŌĆō100 mm | N/A (contact) | 0.5ŌĆō10 mm | 10ŌĆō200 mm |
| **Power source** | Electric motor | Electric current | Vacuum pump | Compressed air |
| **Holding with power loss** | Yes (toggle) | No (EM) / Yes (PM) | No | No (unless spring) |
| **Sterilizable** | Yes (SS) | Yes (with care) | Yes (silicone) | Yes (SS) |
| **Speed** | Medium | Fast (1 ms) | Fast (50 ms) | Very fast (10ŌĆō30 ms) |
| **Workpiece material** | Any | Ferromagnetic only | Smooth, non-porous | Any |
| **Cost** | Medium | LowŌĆōMedium | Medium | Low |
| **Force control** | Excellent (with sensor) | Medium (current control) | Poor (vacuum level only) | Medium (pressure reg.) |
| **Position accuracy** | High (0.01 mm) | N/A | Low | Medium |
| **Cleanroom compatible** | Yes (no exhaust) | Yes | Yes (with filter) | With filtration |
| **MRI compatible** | Difficult (electric) | Yes (PM only) | Yes (with care) | Yes (no metal) |
| **Failure mode** | Lock (toggle) | Drop (EM) | Drop | Spring open/close |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Common Question:** "Compare magnetic, vacuum, and pneumatic grippers." Use this matrix as a template ŌĆö fill in 4ŌĆō5 key rows with brief justification.

---

## 3.7 KTU-Numerical: Multi-Concept Problem

### Problem
A surgical robot gripper uses a slider-crank mechanism with $r = 20$ mm, $l = 60$ mm, finger length $L_f = 40$ mm, and finger moment arm $a = 25$ mm. The motor delivers $T = 1.5$ N┬Ęm at $\omega = 80$ rad/s. The efficiency is 0.85. At $\theta = 45┬░$:

**(a)** Find the slider velocity.
**(b)** Find the gripping force per jaw.

### Solution

**Step 1: Loop closure**
$$r\sin\theta = l\sin\phi$$
$$20 \sin 45┬░ = 60 \sin\phi$$
$$\sin\phi = \frac{20 \times 0.7071}{60} = 0.2357$$
$$\phi = 13.63┬░$$

**Step 2: Velocity ratio**
$$VR = -\frac{r\sin(\theta+\phi)}{\cos\phi} = -\frac{20 \sin(58.63┬░)}{\cos(13.63┬░)} = -\frac{20 \times 0.8536}{0.9719} = -17.56 \text{ mm} = -0.01756 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Slider velocity**
$$\dot{x} = |VR| \cdot \omega = 0.01756 \times 80 = 1.405 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4: Slider force from power balance**
$$F_{slider} = \frac{T \cdot \omega \cdot \eta}{|\dot{x}|} = \frac{1.5 \times 80 \times 0.85}{1.405} = 72.6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Gripping force per jaw**
$$F_{grip} = F_{slider} \cdot \frac{a}{L_f} = 72.6 \times \frac{25}{40} = 45.4 \text{ N}$$

**Result:** Slider velocity = 1.405 m/s, Gripping force = **45.4 N per jaw**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Significance:** 45 N per jaw (90 N total for 2 jaws) is appropriate for holding a surgical needle (typically requires 2ŌĆō5 N clamping force) ŌĆö giving a **safety factor of ~18├Ś**, suitable for delicate tissue manipulation.
