## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Module 1]**
*Define a robotic end effector. List four types of grippers used in medical robotics with one application each.*

#### Model Answer (Expected Length: 8ŌĆō10 lines)

**Definition:** An end effector is a device attached to the wrist of a robotic manipulator, designed to interact with the environment to perform a specific task such as grasping, cutting, or sensing.

**Four types of grippers and applications:**

| Gripper Type | Medical Application |
|--------------|---------------------|
| **Mechanical gripper (slider-crank)** | Grasping and manipulating surgical needles during suturing |
| **Magnetic gripper** | Non-contact navigation of capsule endoscopes in the GI tract |
| **Vacuum gripper** | Handling of contact lenses and sterile packaging in hospital automation |
| **Pneumatic gripper** | Pick-and-place of vials and test tubes in laboratory automation |

**[3 marks: 1 for definition + 0.5 ├Ś 4 for types and applications]**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Module 1]**
*Explain the principle of operation of a vacuum gripper. What is meant by sealing efficiency?*

#### Model Answer (Expected Length: 8ŌĆō10 lines)

A vacuum gripper holds an object using **pressure differential** between atmospheric pressure (outside) and reduced pressure (inside the suction cup). Atmospheric pressure pushes the object against the cup, generating a holding force:

$$F = (P_{atm} - P_{vac}) \cdot A \cdot \eta_{seal}$$

**Components:** Suction cup (silicone/rubber), vacuum source (Venturi ejector or pump), control valve, and check valve.

**Sealing efficiency** $\eta_{seal}$ is the ratio of the actual holding force to the ideal (theoretical) holding force, accounting for:
- Surface roughness
- Air leakage past the cup edge
- Cup deformation
- Workpiece porosity

Typical values: 0.7ŌĆō0.9 for smooth surfaces; 0.5ŌĆō0.6 for rough surfaces.

**[3 marks: 1.5 for principle + 1 for components + 0.5 for sealing efficiency]**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A ŌĆö Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Module 1, Q1(a)/(b) Internal Choice Set A]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the working of a slider-crank gripper mechanism. Derive the expression for the velocity ratio.

**(b) [7 marks]** A slider-crank gripper has crank length $r = 30$ mm, connecting rod $l = 90$ mm, and finger link $L_f = 60$ mm pivoted at $a = 40$ mm from the slider. The crank rotates at $\theta = 75┬░$ from the slider axis. If the motor torque is $T = 3$ N┬Ęm and rotational speed is $\omega = 120$ rad/s with overall efficiency $\eta = 0.88$:
   (i) Calculate the velocity of the slider.
   (ii) Calculate the gripping force per jaw.
   (iii) Comment on the suitability of this force for surgical needle holding.

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) ŌĆö Slider-Crank Gripper Working & Velocity Ratio

**Working Principle:**

A slider-crank gripper converts the rotational motion of a motor-driven crank into linear motion of a slider, which then drives a pivoted finger to open/close the jaws.

[Refer to Section 4.2 for schematic]

**Components:**
- **Crank (1):** Rotates about a fixed pivot, length $r$
- **Connecting rod (2):** Links crank to slider, length $l$
- **Slider (3):** Translates along a fixed linear guide
- **Finger (4):** Pivots about fixed point $O$, length $L_f$, contact with jaw at distance $L_f$ from pivot

**Working Cycle:**
1. Motor drives crank in rotation
2. Connecting rod converts rotation to oscillation
3. Slider moves linearly (reciprocates)
4. Slider's pin pushes/pulls the finger, pivoting it
5. Jaw opens or closes accordingly

**Velocity Ratio Derivation:**

Loop-closure equation (geometric constraint):
$$r\sin\theta = l\sin\phi$$

Differentiating w.r.t. time:
$$r\cos\theta \cdot \dot{\theta} = l\cos\phi \cdot \dot{\phi}$$

Position of slider:
$$x = r\cos\theta + l\cos\phi$$

Differentiating:
$$\dot{x} = -r\sin\theta \cdot \dot{\theta} - l\sin\phi \cdot \dot{\phi}$$

Substituting $\dot{\phi} = \frac{r\cos\theta \cdot \dot{\theta}}{l\cos\phi}$:

$$\dot{x} = -r\sin\theta \cdot \dot{\theta} - l\sin\phi \cdot \frac{r\cos\theta \cdot \dot{\theta}}{l\cos\phi}$$

$$\dot{x} = -r\dot{\theta}\left(\sin\theta + \frac{\sin\phi \cos\theta}{\cos\phi}\right)$$

$$\dot{x} = -r\dot{\theta} \cdot \frac{\sin\theta\cos\phi + \sin\phi\cos\theta}{\cos\phi}$$

$$\dot{x} = -\frac{r\dot{\theta} \sin(\theta + \phi)}{\cos\phi}$$

Therefore, the velocity ratio is:
$$\boxed{VR = \frac{\dot{x}}{\dot{\theta}} = -\frac{r\sin(\theta + \phi)}{\cos\phi}}$$

**[7 marks: 2 for sketch + description, 5 for derivation]**

---

### Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Solution

**Step 1: Loop closure to find $\phi$**
$$r\sin\theta = l\sin\phi$$
$$30 \sin 75┬░ = 90 \sin\phi$$
$$30 \times 0.9659 = 90 \sin\phi$$
$$28.98 = 90 \sin\phi$$
$$\sin\phi = 0.3220$$
$$\phi = 18.78┬░$$

**Step 2: Velocity ratio**
$$VR = -\frac{r\sin(\theta + \phi)}{\cos\phi} = -\frac{30 \sin(75┬░ + 18.78┬░)}{\cos 18.78┬░}$$
$$VR = -\frac{30 \sin(93.78┬░)}{\cos 18.78┬░} = -\frac{30 \times 0.9977}{0.9463} = -31.63 \text{ mm} = -0.03163 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Slider velocity**
$$\dot{x} = |VR| \cdot \omega = 0.03163 \times 120 = 3.796 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4: Slider force from power balance**
$$F_{slider} = \frac{T \cdot \omega \cdot \eta}{|\dot{x}|} = \frac{3 \times 120 \times 0.88}{3.796} = \frac{316.8}{3.796} = 83.46 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Gripping force per jaw**
$$F_{grip} = F_{slider} \cdot \frac{a}{L_f} = 83.46 \times \frac{40}{60} = 55.64 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6: Comment on Surgical Suitability**

A 55.6 N gripping force per jaw (111.3 N total) is **highly suitable for surgical needle holding**:
- Typical suture needle clamping requirement: 2ŌĆō10 N
- Safety factor achieved: 111.3 / 10 = **11.1├Ś**
- This generous margin prevents needle slippage during suturing while avoiding tissue damage from excessive force
- The slider velocity of 3.8 m/s is acceptable for fast jaw motion (cycle time ~10 ms)

**[7 marks: 2 for (i), 2 for (ii), 3 for (iii)]**

---

### Question B ŌĆö Choice 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Module 1, Q2(a)/(b) Internal Choice Set B]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the construction and working of a magnetic gripper. Derive the expression for the holding force of an electromagnet on a flat ferromagnetic plate, in terms of number of turns $N$, current $I$, air gap $l_g$, and pole area $A$.

**(b) [7 marks]** An electromagnetic gripper uses a U-shaped core with $N = 800$ turns, pole area $A = 6 \text{ cm}^2$, and air gap $l_g = 1.5$ mm per pole. The current is $I = 1.5$ A. Calculate:
   (i) Magnetic flux density in the air gap
   (ii) Total holding force on a flat steel plate
   (iii) Holding force if the air gap is reduced to 0.75 mm per pole
   (iv) Comment on the safety implications for medical use.

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) ŌĆö Magnetic Gripper Construction, Working, and Derivation

**Construction:**

[Refer to Section 4.5 for block diagram]

A magnetic gripper consists of:
- **Iron core (yoke):** U-shaped, made of high-permeability silicon steel or soft iron
- **Coil:** Insulated copper wire wound around the core, $N$ turns
- **Pole faces:** Two flat surfaces at the ends of the U, area $A$ each
- **Power supply:** DC source with current control
- **Optional:** Permanent magnet in hybrid designs

**Working Principle:**

When current $I$ flows through the coil, it produces a magnetomotive force $\mathcal{F} = NI$. This drives magnetic flux $\Phi$ through the iron core, across the air gap, and through the ferromagnetic workpiece. The flux lines return through the second air gap and the other leg of the core.

The flux in the air gap creates a magnetic field $B$, which exerts an attractive force on the pole faces and the workpiece. The holding force is:

$$F = \frac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}$$

**Derivation:**

**Step 1: Field in air gap (Ampere's law)**

Applying Ampere's law around the magnetic circuit (assuming iron permeability $\mu_{iron} \to \infty$, so all MMF drops in the air gap):
$$H_{gap} \cdot l_g = NI$$
$$H_{gap} = \frac{NI}{l_g}$$

For a two-pole system (U-shaped), the air gap appears twice:
$$H_{gap} = \frac{NI}{2 l_g} \text{ (per pole)}$$

But for a single air gap calculation (in SI, with $l_g$ being the total effective air gap):
$$B = \mu_0 H = \frac{\mu_0 N I}{l_g}$$

**Step 2: Maxwell stress**

The magnetic pressure (Maxwell stress) on the pole face:
$$p = \frac{B^2}{2\mu_0}$$

**Step 3: Force on pole face**

Force = pressure ├Ś area:
$$F = p \cdot A = \frac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0} = \frac{(\mu_0 NI/l_g)^2 \cdot A}{2\mu_0}$$

**Step 4: Final expression**

$$\boxed{F = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 I^2 A}{2 l_g^2}}$$

**[7 marks: 2 for construction/working, 5 for derivation]**

---

### Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Solution

**Step 1: Magnetic flux density in air gap**

For a two-pole U-core, total air gap = $2 l_g = 2 \times 1.5 = 3$ mm = 0.003 m.

$$H = \frac{NI}{2l_g} = \frac{800 \times 1.5}{0.003} = \frac{1200}{0.003} = 4 \times 10^5 \text{ A/m}$$

$$B = \mu_0 H = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 4 \times 10^5 = 0.5027 \text{ T}$$

**Step 2: Total holding force**

Using the formula with total air gap:
$$F = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 I^2 A}{2 l_g^2} \text{ (single pole equivalent)}$$

For a U-core with two gaps, force on the workpiece is **twice** the single-pole force (both poles attract):

$$F_{single} = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 I^2 A}{2 (2l_g)^2} = \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 800^2 \times 1.5^2 \times 6 \times 10^{-4}}{2 \times 0.003^2}$$

Compute numerator:
$$4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 6.4 \times 10^5 \times 2.25 \times 6 \times 10^{-4} = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 864 = 1.086 \times 10^{-3}$$

Compute denominator:
$$2 \times 9 \times 10^{-6} = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$F_{single} = \frac{1.086 \times 10^{-3}}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} = 60.32 \text{ N}$$

Total force from both poles:
$$F_{total} = 2 \times 60.32 = 120.6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Holding force at $l_g = 0.75$ mm**

Since $F \propto 1/l_g^2$, halving the gap increases force by 4├Ś:
$$F_{new} = F_{old} \times \left(\frac{l_{g,old}}{l_{g,new}}\right)^2 = 120.6 \times \left(\frac{1.5}{0.75}\right)^2 = 120.6 \times 4 = 482.4 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Medical Safety Comment**

- **At 120.6 N:** This is safe for grasping a small ferromagnetic instrument (~12 kg holding capacity). However, **EM gripper fails unsafe** ŌĆö if power is lost, the object drops suddenly. **This is unacceptable in surgery** where sudden release of a needle or instrument near tissue could be catastrophic.
- **At 482.4 N:** While force is increased, the **inverse-square dependence on $l_g$** means any small gap variation causes huge force fluctuations ŌĆö making precise control difficult.
- **Medical recommendation:** Use **permanent magnet (PM) or hybrid (PM+EM) designs** for fail-safe hold. Use EM only in controlled, non-critical applications like external magnetic navigation (capsule endoscopy) where the magnetic field is generated outside the body.

**[7 marks: 1.5 for (i), 2 for (ii), 1.5 for (iii), 2 for (iv)]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in KTU Examinations (Lose 2ŌĆō3 Marks Each):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting efficiency $\eta$ in power balance:** Many students write $F \cdot v = T \cdot \omega$ without including the mechanical efficiency $\eta < 1$. This inflates the gripping force unrealistically. Always include $\eta = 0.8ŌĆō0.9$.
>
> 2. **Sign errors in velocity ratio:** The negative sign in $VR = -r\sin(\theta+\phi)/\cos\phi$ has physical meaning (direction of motion), but students often drop it without comment, leading to wrong force direction in FBD.
>
> 3. **Confusing pressure angle with toggle angle:** In cam grippers, $\alpha$ is between the force on follower and the direction of follower motion. In toggle grippers, $\alpha$ is between the toggle link and the line of output force. Same Greek letter, different meanings!
>
> 4. **Forgetting $A$ in magnetic force formula:** $F = B^2 A / (2\mu_0)$ ŌĆö students often write $F = B^2/(2\mu_0)$ (force per unit area) but compute force. Always check units!
>
> 5. **Pneumatic cylinder force ŌĆō ignoring rod area:** For retract stroke, the effective area is $A_{piston} - A_{rod}$, not $A_{piston}$. Forgetting this makes the retract force calculation 1.5ŌĆō2├Ś too high.
>
> 6. **Vacuum gauge vs absolute pressure:** When $P_{vac} = 30$ kPa is given, is it absolute or gauge? **Always ask.** For force calculation, you need the differential $P_{atm} - P_{vac}$. A 30 kPa absolute vacuum gives 71 kPa differential; a ŌłÆ30 kPa gauge vacuum gives 30 kPa differential ŌĆö 2.4├Ś difference!
>
> 7. **Wrong number of air gaps in EM force:** A U-core has **2** air gaps. Some students use 1 (forgetting the return path) and get twice the actual force. A horseshoe electromagnet with a single air gap has only 1 gap.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Quick-Recall Bullet Summary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION CHECKLIST ŌĆö Master these for KTU ESE.**

#### Mechanisms & Transmission
- Ō£ģ **Mechanism** = assembly of links + joints that transmits motion/force
- Ō£ģ **Slider-crank** converts rotation ŌåÆ reciprocation
- Ō£ģ **Toggle linkage** provides force amplification: $F_{out} = F_{in}/\tan\alpha$
- Ō£ģ **Cam** provides motion programmed by cam profile shape

#### End Effectors
- Ō£ģ **End effector** = tool on robot's wrist (gripper, scalpel, sensor)
- Ō£ģ Medical end effectors must be **sterilizable, biocompatible, force-sensed**

#### Mechanical Grippers
- Ō£ģ **Slider-crank gripper:** velocity ratio $VR = -r\sin(\theta+\phi)/\cos\phi$
- Ō£ģ **Toggle advantage:** $\alpha \to 0┬░$ ŌåÆ $F_{out} \to \infty$ (practical limit 3┬░ŌĆō5┬░)
- Ō£ģ **Cam gripper:** motion depends on cam profile (constant vel., SHM, cycloidal)
- Ō£ģ **Pressure angle $\alpha$** must be $< 30┬░$ to avoid cam jamming

#### Magnetic Grippers
- Ō£ģ **EM force:** $F = \mu_0 N^2 I^2 A / (2 l_g^2)$ ŌĆö inverse square with air gap
- Ō£ģ **PM force:** $F \approx B_r^2 A / (4\mu_0)$
- Ō£ģ **EM fails unsafe** (power loss ŌåÆ drop); **PM fails safe** (always holds)
- Ō£ģ Works on **ferromagnetic** materials only

#### Vacuum Grippers
- Ō£ģ **Force:** $F = \Delta P \cdot A \cdot \eta_{seal}$
- Ō£ģ **Atmospheric pressure** = 101.325 kPa; typical vacuum = 20ŌĆō30 kPa absolute
- Ō£ģ Requires **smooth, non-porous** surface for good seal
- Ō£ģ Components: cup, vacuum source (Venturi), solenoid valve, check valve

#### Pneumatic Grippers
- Ō£ģ **Cylinder force:** $F = P \cdot A$ (extend), $F = P \cdot (A - A_{rod})$ (retract)
- Ō£ģ **Supply pressure:** typically 0.4ŌĆō0.8 MPa (4ŌĆō8 bar)
- Ō£ģ **Double-acting** = air both ways; **single-acting** = spring return
- Ō£ģ Use **FRL unit** (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator) for clean air

#### Numerical Problem Patterns
- Ō£ģ **Force amplification problems:** Use $F_{out}/F_{in} = 1/(2\tan\alpha)$ for toggle
- Ō£ģ **Power balance:** $T\omega\eta = Fv$ (most common gripper force method)
- Ō£ģ **Geometry first, then kinematics, then dynamics** ŌĆö KTU's preferred solution order

#### Key Safety Factors in Medical Robotics
- Ō£ģ Gripper force vs. tissue damage threshold: 0.5 N max for delicate tissue (brain)
- Ō£ģ Safety factor of 5ŌĆō10├Ś for surgical gripper selection
- Ō£ģ **Fail-safe design:** Power loss should result in safe state (release, not crush)

#### Numbers to Memorize
- Ō£ģ $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m = 1.2566 ├Ś 10Ōü╗ŌüČ H/m
- Ō£ģ $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa = 1.01325 bar
- Ō£ģ NdFeB magnet: $B_r \approx 1.4$ T
- Ō£ģ Steel-rubber friction: $\mu \approx 0.7$ŌĆō$1.0$
- Ō£ģ Toggle limit angle: $\alpha_{min} = 3┬░$ŌĆō$5┬░$
- Ō£ģ Cam pressure angle limit: $\alpha_{max} = 30┬░$

### One-Sentence Definition Bank (For 1-Mark Questions)

> Use these for quick recall:

- **Mechanism:** Assembly of rigid links and joints transmitting motion and force.
- **End effector:** Tool at the robot wrist to perform the task.
- **Slider-crank gripper:** A gripper using a 4-bar slider-crank to convert rotation to gripping motion.
- **Toggle gripper:** A gripper using a toggle linkage for high force amplification near the toggle position.
- **Cam gripper:** A gripper driven by a profiled cam acting on a follower.
- **Magnetic gripper:** A gripper using permanent or electromagnets to attract ferromagnetic workpieces.
- **Vacuum gripper:** A gripper using pressure differential (suction) to hold objects.
- **Pneumatic gripper:** A gripper actuated by compressed air through cylinders or vanes.
- **Sealing efficiency:** Ratio of actual to theoretical vacuum holding force.
- **Pressure angle:** Angle between follower force and follower motion direction in cam mechanisms.

### Standard KTU ESE Answer Structure (For Full Marks)

For any 14-mark question, follow this structure:
1. **Diagram/Sketch** (2ŌĆō3 marks)
2. **Definition and Working Principle** (2 marks)
3. **Mathematical Derivation** (3ŌĆō4 marks)
4. **Numerical Solution with steps** (3ŌĆō4 marks)
5. **Engineering Comment / Application** (1ŌĆō2 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 1 Notes ŌĆö Mechanisms, End Effectors, and Grippers.**
> **Practice the derivations in Section 3.6 and the numerical problems in Section 3.7 for ESE confidence.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Specifications of robot
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT501 - MEDICAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Specifications of robot

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme and NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education, **Robot Specifications** constitute the comprehensive set of quantitative and qualitative technical parameters that define the kinematic, dynamic, and operational capabilities of a robotic manipulator. For medical robots, these specifications are not merely engineering attributes; they are **clinical performance envelopes** governed by international safety standards such as **ISO 13482** (Personal care robots), **IEC 61508** (Functional safety), and **ISO 14971** (Risk management for medical devices).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 1):** A medical robot is specified not just by what it *can do*, but by the *precision and safety bounds* within which it operates inside the human body.

A robot specification sheet typically enumerates the following primary parameters:

| # | Specification | Core Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Degrees of Freedom (DOF) | Number of independent positional/orientation variables |
| 2 | Payload Capacity | Maximum end-effector load (N or kg) |
| 3 | Workspace / Reach | Spatial envelope of reachable points (m┬│ or m) |
| 4 | Speed | Maximum TCP velocity (m/s) and joint velocity (rad/s) |
| 5 | Accuracy | Ability to reach an *absolute commanded* position |
| 6 | Repeatability | Ability to return to a *previously taught* position |
| 7 | Resolution | Smallest achievable incremental motion |
| 8 | Stiffness | Resistance to deflection under load (N/m) |
| 9 | Backlash | Lost motion due to gear clearances (mm or rad) |
| 10 | Power Source | Pneumatic, hydraulic, or electric drive |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine you are performing a delicate **brain surgery** using a robotic arm. Think of the robot like a **surgical pencil held by an ultra-steady mechanical hand**:

- The **Degrees of Freedom** are like the number of joints in your own fingers, wrist, and shoulder ŌĆö they determine how *flexibly* you can orient the pencil from any angle.
- The **Workspace** is the bubble of space around the patient where your pencil can physically reach. If the bubble is too small, you cannot operate on the left side of the brain; if too large, the arm becomes bulky and obtrusive.
- The **Repeatability** is like your muscle memory ŌĆö you can touch the same spot on the patient again and again within 0.1 mm. This is more critical than *accuracy* in surgery because surgeons work with pre-operative images (relative positions matter more than absolute).
- The **Payload** is the maximum weight the mechanical hand can hold (scalpel, endoscope, drill) without trembling.
- The **Resolution** is the smallest wiggle your pencil can make ŌĆö like a violinist's micro-tremolo.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** A surgical robot like the **da Vinci Xi** has 7 DOF, 0.02 mm repeatability, and a fully enclosed (isocentric) workspace, allowing a surgeon to stitch a grape back together ŌĆö a feat of both specification and engineering.

## 1.3 Why Specifications Differ for Medical Robots

Unlike industrial robots that optimize for *speed and payload*, medical robots prioritize:

1. **Sub-millimetric accuracy** (often < 0.1 mm)
2. **Master-slave force scaling** (motion scaling ratios like 3:1 or 5:1)
3. **Haptic transparency** ŌĆö the surgeon must *feel* tissue resistance
4. **Sterilizability and biocompatibility** of materials
5. **MRI / CT compatibility** (non-ferromagnetic alloys)
6. **Redundancy and fail-safe** actuation (ISO 13849)

These constraints are the reason why the **da Vinci, MAKO, ROSA, and CyberKnife** systems command multi-million-dollar price tags ŌĆö they are the *Ferrari* of robotic specifications.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 10 Pillars of Robot Specifications ŌĆö Structured Logic

Let us deconstruct each specification, its operational *why*, and its *how* in the medical context.

### Pillar 1: Degrees of Freedom (DOF)

A rigid body in 3D space requires exactly **6 DOF** to be fully positioned and oriented: 3 translations (X, Y, Z) and 3 rotations (Roll, Pitch, Yaw). Medical robots often have **7 DOF** to avoid kinematic singularities and allow the surgeon to "elbow-up" the manipulator inside a confined cavity (e.g., cardiac surgery).

- **How counted:** Each independent joint (revolute or prismatic) contributes one DOF.
- **Why it matters:** Under-actuated robots (<6 DOF) cannot orient an end-effector arbitrarily; over-actuated robots (>6 DOF) provide redundancy for obstacle avoidance.

### Pillar 2: Payload Capacity

Medical robots carry lightweight instruments (typically **0.5 kg to 3 kg**) ŌĆö far below industrial robots (up to 1000 kg). However, the *effective payload* must account for the **weight of cabling, force sensors, and sterile drapes**.

### Pillar 3: Workspace (Work Envelope)

The workspace is the 3D volume swept by the Tool Center Point (TCP). Four canonical workspace shapes correspond to the four primary robot configurations:

| Configuration | Workspace Shape | Medical Application |
|---|---|---|
| Cartesian / Gantry | Rectangular box | Linear accelerators, CyberKnife |
| Cylindrical | Hollow cylinder | Older imaging C-arms |
| Spherical (Polar) | Spherical sector | Rare in modern medical robots |
| Articulated (Revolute) | Doughnut/torus | da Vinci, MAKO, ROSA |
| SCARA | Horizontal disk | Lab automation, not surgical |
| Parallel (Delta) | Dome above base | High-speed lab pick-and-place |

### Pillar 4 & 5: Speed and Acceleration

Measured at the TCP. Medical robots deliberately limit velocity to **Ōēż 25 mm/s** at the slave tip during teleoperation (a regulatory safety bound) but may reach **> 1 m/s** in autonomous image-guided modes.

### Pillar 6: Accuracy

$$A = \frac{1}{2}(X_{\text{err}} + Y_{\text{err}} + Z_{\text{err}})$$

Accuracy is the difference between the *commanded position* and the *actual attained position* in world coordinates. It is a function of:

- **Kinematic errors** (link length tolerances, joint offsets)
- **Calibration errors**
- **Thermal drift** (critical in long neurosurgical procedures)
- **Compliance errors** (deflection under tool weight)

### Pillar 7: Repeatability

$$R = \overline{r} \pm 3\sigma_r \quad \text{where} \quad \overline{r} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} r_i$$

It is defined as the radius of the smallest sphere that contains **99.7% (3Žā)** of all attained positions when repeatedly commanded to the same point. Repeatability is *always better* than accuracy ŌĆö a robot can be repeatable without being accurate, but cannot be accurate without being repeatable.

### Pillar 8: Resolution

$$R_{\text{res}} = \frac{\text{Joint travel}}{\text{Encoder counts} \times \text{Gear ratio}}$$

The smallest discrete movement the controller can issue. Modern optical encoders provide resolutions of **Ōēź 20 bits per revolution**.

### Pillar 9 & 10: Stiffness and Backlash

$$k = \frac{F}{\delta} \quad [\text{N/m}]$$

Where $\delta$ is the deflection under applied force $F$. In orthopedic robots (e.g., MAKOplasty), **high stiffness (> 50 N/mm)** is essential to prevent tool-bending during bone cutting. **Backlash** manifests as dead-zone error and is minimized using pre-loaded harmonic-drive reducers (common in the da Vinci).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **minimum formula set** required to solve any KTU Module 1 numerical problem on robot specifications.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Unit | Application / Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DOF via Gr├╝blerŌĆōKutzbach | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | dimensionless | $n$ = links, $j_1$ = 1-DOF joints, $j_2$ = 2-DOF joints |
| 2 | DOF in 3D space | $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | dimensionless | Spatial mechanism version |
| 3 | Required DOF (rigid body) | $M_{\text{req}} = 6$ | dimensionless | 3 translations + 3 rotations |
| 4 | Accuracy | $A = \frac{1}{2}(X_{\text{err}} + Y_{\text{err}} + Z_{\text{err}})$ | mm | Half the sum of axis errors |
| 5 | Repeatability | $R = \bar{r} + 3\sigma_r$ | mm | Mean + 3 standard deviations |
| 6 | Resolution | $R_{\text{res}} = \dfrac{L_{\text{joint}}}{N_{\text{enc}} \cdot G}$ | mm or rad | $N_{\text{enc}}$ = encoder counts, $G$ = gear ratio |
| 7 | Stiffness | $k = F / \delta$ | N/m | Force per unit deflection |
| 8 | Max Reach | $L_{\max} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i$ | m | Sum of link lengths |
| 9 | Workspace Volume (Spherical) | $V = \frac{2}{3}\pi (R^3 - r^3)$ | m┬│ | $R$ = max, $r$ = min reach |
| 10 | Motion Scaling | $\dot{x}_{\text{slave}} = k_s \cdot \dot{x}_{\text{master}}$ | m/s | $k_s$ typically $0.1$ to $0.33$ |
| 11 | Force Scaling | $F_{\text{slave}} = k_f \cdot F_{\text{master}}$ | N | Master-side force feedback ratio |
| 12 | Repeatability vs Accuracy | $R \approx \frac{1}{2} A$ to $\frac{1}{3} A$ | ŌĆö | Empirical industrial rule |
| 13 | Velocity Limit (ISO) | $v_{\text{TCP}} \le 25 \text{ mm/s}$ | mm/s | Safety bound for surgical robots |
| 14 | Workspace (Revolute 2-link) | $x^2 + y^2 \le (a_1 + a_2)^2$ | m┬▓ | Planar reachability disc |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In clinical practice, these specifications cascade into life-critical decisions:

- **Neurosurgery (ROSA, NeuroArm):** 6-DOF with sub-millimetric accuracy is non-negotiable because a 1 mm error may sever the corticospinal tract.
- **Orthopedics (MAKO):** High stiffness and 5-DOF milling accuracy preserve the bone-implant interface.
- **Laparoscopy (da Vinci):** 7-DOF redundancy prevents the instrument shafts from clashing inside the patient's abdomen.
- **Radiosurgery (CyberKnife):** 6-DOF linear accelerator with 0.1 mm accuracy targets spinal tumors while compensating for breathing motion.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Gr├╝blerŌĆōKutzbach Formula for Degrees of Freedom

### Step 1: Set up the topological framework

Consider a planar mechanism (2D) with $n$ rigid links (including the ground link) and $j_1$ lower-pair joints (each contributing 1 DOF) and $j_2$ higher-pair joints (each contributing 2 DOF). We wish to find the mobility $M$ ŌĆö the number of independent inputs needed to fully define the configuration.

### Step 2: Count total unconstrained DOF

Each free rigid body in a plane has $3$ DOF (2 translations + 1 rotation). For $n$ links:

$$
F_{\text{total}} = 3n
$$

### Step 3: Subtract joint constraints

Each lower-pair joint removes 2 DOF (allows 1 relative motion). Each higher-pair joint removes 1 DOF (allows 2 relative motions).

$$
F_{\text{removed}} = 2 j_1 + 1 j_2
$$

### Step 4: Also remove the ground link's mobility

The ground link has 3 DOF that are *not* part of the mechanism's mobility. So we subtract 3:

$$
M = 3n - (2 j_1 + 1 j_2) - 3
$$

### Step 5: Simplify to the canonical form

$$
M = 3(n - 1) - 2 j_1 - 1 j_2
$$

This is the **planar Gr├╝blerŌĆōKutzbach formula** ŌĆö derived line by line above.

### Step 6: Extend to 3D (Spatial) Mechanisms

In 3D, a free rigid body has 6 DOF. A 1-DOF joint removes 5 DOF, a 2-DOF joint removes 4, and so on. Replacing the constants:

$$
M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - 1j_5
$$

This is the **spatial Kutzbach criterion** used for all modern medical manipulators.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship Between Resolution, Encoder Counts and Joint Travel

### Step 1: Define the joint angular range

A revolute joint spans an angular range $\theta_{\text{range}}$ (in radians).

### Step 2: Define encoder resolution

A quadrature optical encoder produces $N_{\text{counts}}$ pulses per revolution (per channel). After quadrature decoding:

$$
N_{\text{quad}} = 4 \cdot N_{\text{counts}}
$$

### Step 3: Define gear reduction

The gear ratio $G$ (motor revolutions per joint revolution) scales the apparent resolution:

$$
R_{\text{res, rad}} = \frac{2\pi}{N_{\text{quad}} \cdot G} = \frac{\pi}{2 N_{\text{counts}} \cdot G}
$$

### Step 4: Convert to linear resolution at a link of length $L$

$$
R_{\text{res, linear}} = L \cdot R_{\text{res, rad}} = \frac{\pi L}{2 N_{\text{counts}} \cdot G}
$$

### Step 5: Worked Example ŌĆö da Vinci instrument wrist

Given $L = 5$ mm, $N_{\text{counts}} = 8192$, $G = 100$:

$$
R_{\text{res}} = \frac{\pi \times 0.005}{2 \times 8192 \times 100} = \frac{0.01571}{1{,}638{,}400} \approx 9.6 \text{ nm}
$$

This explains why the da Vinci can manipulate a suture finer than a human hair.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Accuracy vs Repeatability ŌĆö The Statistical Meaning

### Step 1: Position error definition

For $N$ repeated commands to the same target point $P_{\text{cmd}}$, the attained positions are $P_i$, $i = 1, 2, \dots, N$.

### Step 2: Mean attained position

$$
\bar{P} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i
$$

### Step 3: Accuracy (bias error)

$$
A = \Vert P_{\text{cmd}} - \bar{P} \Vert
$$

This is the **bias** of the system ŌĆö a systematic error that *can be calibrated out*.

### Step 4: Repeatability (precision error)

$$
R = 3 \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_z^2}
$$

Where each $\sigma$ is the standard deviation of attained positions along one axis. The factor 3 corresponds to the **3-sigma rule** (99.7% confidence).

### Step 5: Final error budget

$$
E_{\text{total}} = A + R
$$

A surgical robot must satisfy $E_{\text{total}} < 1 \text{ mm}$ for spinal applications and $< 0.5 \text{ mm}$ for intracranial applications.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Specs of Common Medical Robots

| Robot | DOF | Payload | Repeatability | Workspace | Key Clinical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| da Vinci Xi | 7 (slave) | 1.5 kg | 0.02 mm | 360┬░ around port | Laparoscopy |
| MAKO RIO | 5 | 4.5 kg | < 0.5 mm | Spherical, 60 cm radius | Knee/hip arthroplasty |
| ROSA Brain | 6 | 2 kg | 1 mm | 300 ├Ś 200 ├Ś 200 mm | Stereotactic neurosurgery |
| CyberKnife | 6 | ŌĆö | 0.1 mm | 1000 mm LINAC reach | Radiosurgery |
| NeuroArm | 6 + haptics | 0.5 kg | < 0.1 mm | MRI-bore compatible | Microsurgery |
| Da Vinci SP | 7 (single port) | 1 kg | 0.02 mm | 270┬░ cone | Transoral surgery |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Robot Specifications

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Robot Specifications] --> B[Kinematic Specs]
    A --> C[Dynamic Specs]
    A --> D[Static Specs]
    A --> E[Safety Specs]
    
    B --> B1[Degrees of Freedom]
    B --> B2[Workspace Envelope]
    B --> B3[Joint Limits and Range]
    B --> B4[Configuration Type]
    
    C --> C1[Speed Max]
    C --> C2[Acceleration Max]
    C --> C3[Payload Capacity]
    C --> C4[Natural Frequency]
    
    D --> D1[Accuracy]
    D --> D2[Repeatability]
    D --> D3[Resolution]
    D --> D4[Stiffness]
    D --> D5[Backlash]
    
    E --> E1[ISO 13482 Compliance]
    E --> E2[Force Torque Sensing]
    E --> E3[Emergency Stop Time]
    E --> E4[Sterilization Method]
    
    B1 --> F[Clinical Performance Envelope]
    B2 --> F
    D2 --> F
    D3 --> F
    E2 --> F
    
    F --> G[Surgical Outcome]
```

## 4.2 Diagram: Hierarchical Map of the 10 Specification Pillars

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P1[Kinematic Cluster]
        P1A[DOF]
        P1B[Workspace]
        P1C[Reach]
        P1D[Joint Type]
    end
    
    subgraph P2[Dynamic Cluster]
        P2A[Speed]
        P2B[Acceleration]
        P2C[Payload]
    end
    
    subgraph P3[Static Cluster]
        P3A[Accuracy]
        P3B[Repeatability]
        P3C[Resolution]
        P3D[Stiffness]
        P3E[Backlash]
    end
    
    subgraph P4[Operational Cluster]
        P4A[Drive Type]
        P4B[Power Source]
        P4C[Control Bandwidth]
    end
    
    P1 --> P3
    P2 --> P3
    P2 --> P4
    P3 --> Q[Total Performance Metric]
    P4 --> Q
```

## 4.3 Diagram: Accuracy vs Repeatability ŌĆö Target Analogy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Commanded Position P cmd] --> B{Measured Positions}
    B --> C[Mean Position P bar]
    B --> D[Cluster Spread sigma]
    
    C --> E[Accuracy = distance from A to C]
    D --> F[Repeatability = 3 sigma radius]
    
    E --> G[Total Position Error = A plus R]
    F --> G
    
    G --> H[Medical Application Suitability]
    H --> I[Sub mm: Neurosurgery]
    H --> J[Sub 0.1 mm: Microsurgery]
    H --> K[1 to 2 mm: Orthopedics]
```

## 4.4 Diagram: Workspace Configurations of Manipulators

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    W[Robot Workspace Types] --> W1[Cartesian XYZ]
    W --> W2[Cylindrical]
    W --> W3[Spherical Polar]
    W --> W4[Articulated Revolute]
    W --> W5[SCARA]
    W --> W6[Parallel Delta]
    
    W1 --> W1A[Box shaped: Linear accelerator]
    W2 --> W2A[Hollow cylinder: Older C arm]
    W3 --> W3A[Sphere: Rare in medical]
    W4 --> W4A[Torus or doughnut: da Vinci]
    W5 --> W5A[Flat disc: Lab automation]
    W6 --> W6A[Dome: High speed lab work]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above four diagrams provide a **complete functional map** of robot specifications. Each cluster ŌĆö kinematic, dynamic, static, and operational ŌĆö feeds into the *clinical performance envelope* that ultimately determines surgical outcome quality.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Degrees of Freedom (DOF) of a robot. Why is a 7-DOF preferred over 6-DOF in laparoscopic surgical robots? **[3 Marks]** ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Degrees of Freedom (DOF) of a robot is defined as the **number of independent joint motions** required to fully specify the position and orientation of the end-effector in 3D space. A rigid body requires 6 DOF (3 translations + 3 rotations) for general placement; therefore, any 6-DOF robot is said to be *non-redundant*.

A 7-DOF manipulator is preferred in laparoscopic surgical robots such as the **da Vinci** for three reasons:

1. **Singularity avoidance:** At a kinematic singularity, the robot loses one or more DOF, which is unacceptable inside the patient's abdomen. The 7th joint provides a *redundant* degree of freedom to maneuver *out* of singular configurations.
2. **Elbow-up positioning:** Inside the small incision port, the surgeon benefits from lifting the "elbow" of the arm upward to avoid collisions between instrument shafts.
3. **Triangulation of instruments:** Two 7-DOF slave arms converging at the surgical site can be positioned with a wider angular separation, improving the surgeon's depth perception.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark allocation:** Definition = 1 mark, 6-DOF context = 1 mark, three reasons for 7-DOF = 1 mark (any two valid reasons).

---

### Question 2: Distinguish between **accuracy** and **repeatability** of a robot. Why is repeatability more critical than accuracy in image-guided surgery? **[3 Marks]** ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Accuracy | Repeatability |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Ability to reach a *commanded absolute* position | Ability to return to a *previously attained* position |
| Mathematical form | $A = \Vert P_{\text{cmd}} - \bar{P} \Vert$ | $R = 3 \sigma$ of cluster |
| Correction | Bias error ŌĆö *calibratable* | Random error ŌĆö *not calibratable* |
| Typical value (surgical) | 1ŌĆō2 mm | 0.02ŌĆō0.1 mm |

**Why repeatability dominates in image-guided surgery:**

In image-guided procedures, the surgical plan is built from a **pre-operative CT or MRI scan**. The robot is registered to the patient using fiducial markers. Once registered, all subsequent moves are *relative* to this registration. Therefore, the *consistency* of motion (repeatability) matters more than the absolute pointing accuracy. Calibration can correct bias; random scatter cannot be removed by calibration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark allocation:** Distinction table = 2 marks, surgical reasoning = 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / May 2024 Module 1 Choice]`

#### Part (a): Explain the major robot specifications used in medical robotics, classifying them into kinematic, dynamic, and static categories. Give at least two specifications from each category. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

Robot specifications in medical robotics are the engineering parameters that constrain the *clinical performance envelope* of the manipulator. They can be classified as follows:

**(i) Kinematic Specifications (concerned with geometry of motion):**

1. **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** Number of independent joint variables. A 6-DOF robot can position and orient the end-effector arbitrarily in 3D. The da Vinci uses 7 DOF for redundancy.
2. **Workspace / Work Envelope:** The 3D volume swept by the Tool Center Point (TCP). For an articulated robot, the workspace is a *doughnut-shaped* region between the inner sphere (minimum reach) and outer sphere (maximum reach).
3. **Joint Range and Type:** Each revolute (R) or prismatic (P) joint has angular or linear limits (e.g., ┬▒170┬░ for shoulder roll, 0ŌĆō270┬░ for elbow pitch).

**(ii) Dynamic Specifications (concerned with motion and force over time):**

1. **Maximum Speed:** The peak TCP velocity. ISO standards limit surgical slave tips to Ōēż 25 mm/s for safety, while the master can move at 100 mm/s with motion scaling.
2. **Acceleration / Jerk:** Important for tremor filtering in microsurgical robots; jerk-limited trajectories (5th-order polynomials) are used to avoid startling the surgeon.
3. **Payload Capacity:** For a 5 mm laparoscopic instrument weighing 0.4 kg, the wrist must sustain 1 kg of effective load (instrument + sterile drape + cabling).

**(iii) Static Specifications (concerned with position holding under load):**

1. **Accuracy:** Difference between commanded and attained position; typically 0.5ŌĆō2 mm for medical robots.
2. **Repeatability:** 3Žā scatter radius upon repeated commands; 0.02 mm for da Vinci, 0.1 mm for CyberKnife.
3. **Resolution:** Smallest incremental motion; calculated as $R_{\text{res}} = L / (N_{\text{enc}} \cdot G)$.
4. **Stiffness:** Resistance to deflection; > 50 N/mm in orthopedic robots to prevent tool bending during bone milling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark allocation:** Three categories identified = 3 marks, two specs each explained = 3 marks, medical examples = 1 mark.

---

#### Part (b): Using Gr├╝bler's formula, calculate the degrees of freedom of a PUMA 560 manipulator. State your assumptions clearly. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the manipulator topology:**

The PUMA 560 is a 6-DOF articulated manipulator with all revolute joints: **R-R-R-R-R-R** (waist rotation, shoulder pitch, elbow pitch, wrist roll, wrist pitch, wrist yaw).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Count the links:**

Including the ground (base) link:
$$
n = 7
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Count the joints:**

There are 6 revolute joints, each contributing 1 DOF:
$$
j_1 = 6, \quad j_2 = 0
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply Gr├╝bler's spatial formula:**

$$
M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Substitute values:**

$$
M = 6(7-1) - 5(6) - 4(0)
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Simplify:**

$$
M = 6 \times 6 - 30 - 0
$$

$$
M = 36 - 30
$$

$$
M = 6
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Interpretation:**

The PUMA 560 has 6 DOF, which is exactly the *minimum* required to position and orient a rigid body in 3D space (3 translations + 3 rotations). It is therefore a **non-redundant** manipulator and may encounter kinematic singularities inside its workspace.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark allocation:** Topology sketch/description = 2 marks, Gr├╝bler formula stated = 2 marks, substitution = 2 marks, result and interpretation = 1 mark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks ŌĆö Alternative Choice)** ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 / Dec 2024 Module 1]`

#### Part (a): With suitable diagrams, explain the different types of robot workspace configurations. Mention one medical application for each. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The workspace of a robot is the set of all points reachable by its Tool Center Point. There are six canonical workspace shapes:

**1. Cartesian / Gantry (PPP):**
- **Geometry:** A rectangular box defined by the three prismatic joint extents.
- **Volume:** $V = L_x \cdot L_y \cdot L_z$
- **Medical application:** The **CyberKnife** robotic radiosurgery system uses a Cartesian-style linear accelerator to deliver radiation beams from multiple angles.

**2. Cylindrical (RPP):**
- **Geometry:** A hollow cylinder (or cylindrical sector).
- **Volume:** $V = \pi (R^2 - r^2) h$ where $R$ = max reach, $r$ = min reach, $h$ = vertical travel.
- **Medical application:** Older **C-arm fluoroscopy** units approximate cylindrical reach for imaging around the patient.

**3. Spherical / Polar (RRP):**
- **Geometry:** A spherical sector defined by two rotations and one prismatic reach.
- **Volume:** $V = \frac{2}{3}\pi (R^3 - r^3)$
- **Medical application:** Rare in modern surgical robots; historically used in some early **patient-positioning** systems.

**4. Articulated / Revolute (RRR):**
- **Geometry:** A doughnut / torus shape ŌĆö a sphere with a hollow center (where the base and shoulder obstructions lie).
- **Volume:** Approximately $\frac{4}{3}\pi (R^3 - r^3) \cdot \eta$ where $\eta \approx 0.6$ is an empirical fill factor.
- **Medical application:** **da Vinci Xi** surgical system ŌĆö the most widely used articulated medical robot.

**5. SCARA (RRP):**
- **Geometry:** A flat disk at a fixed height.
- **Application:** Primarily **lab automation** (sample handling), not surgical.

**6. Parallel / Delta:**
- **Geometry:** A dome above the base.
- **Application:** **High-speed pick-and-place** in pharmaceutical packaging; rarely used at bedside.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark allocation:** Six configurations = 3 marks, geometries = 2 marks, medical applications = 2 marks.

---

#### Part (b): Discuss accuracy, repeatability, and resolution in robot specifications. How do they relate to each other mathematically, and what is their clinical significance? **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition 1 ŌĆö Accuracy ($A$):**
Accuracy is the *systematic* deviation between the **commanded position** $P_{\text{cmd}}$ and the **mean attained position** $\bar{P}$ after $N$ repeated attempts:

$$
A = \Vert P_{\text{cmd}} - \bar{P} \Vert
$$

It is essentially the **bias** of the system and is influenced by:
- Link length manufacturing tolerances (typically ┬▒ 0.05 mm)
- Calibration residual errors
- Thermal expansion during long procedures
- Compliance of the structure under gravity

**Definition 2 ŌĆö Repeatability ($R$):**
Repeatability is the *random* deviation of attained positions about the mean. It is quantified as **three standard deviations** of the cluster:

$$
R = 3\sigma \quad \text{where} \quad \sigma = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_z^2}
$$

It is influenced by:
- Encoder quantization
- Backlash in gear trains
- Joint friction and stiction
- Cable hysteresis

**Definition 3 ŌĆö Resolution ($R_{\text{res}}$):**
Resolution is the **smallest incremental motion** the controller can command, bounded by:

$$
R_{\text{res}} = \frac{\text{Joint travel}}{N_{\text{enc}} \cdot G}
$$

A typical da Vinci wrist has linear resolution of $\sim 10$ nanometres at the tip.

**Mathematical Relationship:**

$$
R_{\text{res}} \le R \le A
$$

That is, the resolution sets a *lower bound* on achievable repeatability, and accuracy is generally *worse* than repeatability because it adds bias on top of random scatter. Empirically:

$$
R \approx \frac{1}{2} A \quad \text{to} \quad \frac{1}{3} A
$$

**Clinical Significance:**

| Surgery Type | Required Accuracy | Required Repeatability |
|---|---|---|
| Stereotactic neurosurgery | < 1 mm | < 0.3 mm |
| Spinal pedicle screw placement | 1ŌĆō2 mm | < 0.5 mm |
| Knee arthroplasty (MAKO) | < 1 mm | < 0.5 mm |
| Laparoscopic suturing (da Vinci) | 2ŌĆō3 mm | < 0.1 mm |
| Radiosurgery (CyberKnife) | < 0.5 mm | < 0.1 mm |

High *accuracy* allows the robot to align with pre-operative plans, while high *repeatability* ensures the robot does not wander between successive moves. Together, they define the **clinical safety margin** of the device.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark allocation:** Three definitions = 3 marks, mathematical relationships = 2 marks, clinical table = 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Module 1 Specifications Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing accuracy with repeatability.** Remember: *accuracy* is a bias (systematic, calibratable), *repeatability* is a scatter (random, not calibratable).
> 2. **Forgetting to include the ground link** in Gr├╝bler's formula. The $n$ in $3(n-1)$ or $6(n-1)$ must include the fixed base link.
> 3. **Mixing planar and spatial Gr├╝bler formulas.** For 3D mechanisms (like PUMA), use $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2$, not the planar one.
> 4. **Stating workspace as a single number.** Workspace is a *volume* (m┬│) or *reach* (m), not a single coordinate. Always mention the shape (box, cylinder, sphere, torus).
> 5. **Ignoring units.** Resolution, accuracy, and repeatability are all in *mm* (or *rad* for rotary joints). Examiners explicitly deduct for missing units.
> 6. **Forgetting the safety aspect.** Medical robots must be specified under ISO 13482 / IEC 61508. Missing this loses you a "regulatory awareness" mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **DOF** = number of independent joint variables; 6 needed for general 3D placement; 7 preferred in surgical robots for redundancy.
- [x] **Gr├╝bler's Planar Formula:** $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$
- [x] **Gr├╝bler's Spatial Formula:** $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$
- [x] **PUMA 560 DOF = 6**, **da Vinci DOF = 7** (4 arm joints + 3 instrument wrist joints).
- [x] **Accuracy** = bias = systematic = calibratable = $A = \Vert P_{\text{cmd}} - \bar{P} \Vert$
- [x] **Repeatability** = 3Žā = random = not calibratable = $R = 3\sigma$
- [x] **Resolution** = smallest commanded increment = $R_{\text{res}} = L / (N_{\text{enc}} \cdot G)$
- [x] **Stiffness** = force/deflection = $k = F/\delta$ (units: N/m).
- [x] **Workspace types:** Cartesian (box), Cylindrical, Spherical, Articulated (torus), SCARA (disk), Parallel (dome).
- [x] **Articulated (RRR) workspace** is the most common in medical robots (da Vinci, MAKO, ROSA).
- [x] **Payload range for surgical robots** is typically 0.5ŌĆō5 kg (much lower than industrial robots).
- [x] **ISO safety limit** for surgical slave tip velocity: Ōēż 25 mm/s.
- [x] **Motion scaling** ratio for surgical teleoperation: typically 3:1 to 10:1.
- [x] **Force scaling** for haptic feedback: 1:1 to 5:1 (varies by tissue type).
- [x] **Backlash** is minimized using harmonic-drive reducers (zero-backlash gear trains).
- [x] **Typical repeatability:** da Vinci = 0.02 mm, CyberKnife = 0.1 mm, ROSA = 1 mm.
- [x] **Typical accuracy** in image-guided surgery: 0.5ŌĆō2 mm (after registration).
- [x] **Clinical significance** hierarchy: Neurosurgery > Spinal > Orthopedic > Laparoscopic > Diagnostic imaging.
- [x] **Medical robot regulations:** ISO 13482, IEC 61508, ISO 14971, ISO 13849.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Note for KTU 2024 Aspirants:** The specifications of a robot are *not* just numbers on a datasheet ŌĆö they are the **clinical safety envelope** of a device that operates inside the human body. Mastering the trade-off between **accuracy (calibratable bias)** and **repeatability (irreducible scatter)** is the single most important conceptual takeaway of Module 1. Combine this with Gr├╝bler's formula and workspace analysis, and you have the foundation to ace any 14-mark Module 1 question in the KTU 2024 ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drive systems - pneumatic drives, hydraulic drives, mechanical drives, electrical drives, DC servo motors, stepper motors, AC servo motors - salient features and applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT501 - MEDICAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Drive systems - pneumatic drives, hydraulic drives, mechanical drives, electrical drives, DC servo motors, stepper motors, AC servo motors - salient features and applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 ŌĆö DRIVE SYSTEMS IN MEDICAL ROBOTICS

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

In the context of Medical Robotics, a **Drive System** is defined as the controlled energy-conversion and power-transmission subsystem that generates the mechanical motion (linear, rotary, or oscillatory) required by the manipulator, end-effector, or locomotion unit of a medical robot. It accepts a command signal from the controller and produces a precisely regulated force, torque, or displacement at the output.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):**
> *A drive system is a mechatronic assembly comprising an actuator, power-conditioning unit, transmission element, and feedback sensor that transforms an input energy form (electrical, pneumatic, hydraulic, or mechanical) into controlled mechanical work for use in surgical, rehabilitation, or diagnostic robotic applications.*

### 1.2 Functional Classification of Drive Systems

A medical robot's drive system is selected based on payload (typically **0.5 N to 50 N** for surgical robots), required speed (**0.1 mm/s to 500 mm/s** for tool tip), sterilisability, back-drivability, and cleanroom compatibility.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy-density benchmark (KTU High-Yield):**
> - Pneumatic energy density: **Ōēł 0.3 kJ/kg**
> - Hydraulic energy density: **Ōēł 2.0 kJ/kg**
> - Electrical (DC motor) energy density: **Ōēł 0.6 kJ/kg**

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "MuscleŌĆōTendonŌĆōJoint" Mapping

Imagine the human musculoskeletal system. The **muscle** is the *actuator* (it generates force), the **tendon** is the *transmission* (it carries and modulates the force), and the **joint** is the *output link* (it produces the motion you see). In a surgical robot:

- The **muscle** is the prime mover ŌĆö a pneumatic cylinder, a hydraulic piston, or an electric motor.
- The **tendon** is the mechanical transmission ŌĆö gear train, harmonic drive, Bowden cable, or capstan.
- The **joint** is the kinematic link ŌĆö the revolute or prismatic pair of the robot arm.
- The **brain signal** is the controller's PWM/analog command.
- The **proprioceptive nerve** is the encoder/force sensor in the feedback loop.

> [!TIP]
> **Remember:** A drive system without feedback is *open-loop* (e.g., a stepper motor); with feedback it becomes a *servo drive* (e.g., DC servo, AC servo). This distinction is a **guaranteed question** in KTU exams.

### 1.4 Why Drive System Choice is Critical in Medical Robotics

| Criterion | Surgical Robot | Rehabilitation Robot | Diagnostic/Imaging Robot |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Precision required** | Sub-millimeter (0.1 mm) | Centimeter-level | Sub-degree angular |
| **Force range** | 2 N to 10 N (tissue handling) | 50 N to 200 N (limb assistance) | 0.1 N to 5 N (probe positioning) |
| **Back-drivability** | High (safety) | Moderate | Low |
| **Sterilisability** | Mandatory (autoclavable) | Surface-clean only | Sealed enclosure |
| **Preferred drive** | Electrical / DC servo | Pneumatic muscle / hydraulic | Stepper / AC servo |

This table forms the **engineering decision matrix** for selecting a drive technology in any medical robotic platform.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 ŌĆö DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Pneumatic Drives

### 2.1.1 Operating Principle
A pneumatic drive uses **compressed air** (typically at 4 bar to 8 bar, medical grade ISO 8573-1 Class 1.2.1) as the working fluid. The compressed air pushes against a piston, vane, or flexible membrane, converting pneumatic pressure energy into linear or rotary mechanical work.

### 2.1.2 Salient Features
- **High power-to-weight ratio** ŌĆö air is light, allowing compact actuators.
- **Inherently compliant** ŌĆö air is compressible, providing natural safety and overload protection.
- **Clean and sterile** ŌĆö exhaust air does not contaminate the surgical field; ideal for operating theatres.
- **Fast response** ŌĆö actuation time in the order of **5 ms to 50 ms**.
- **Low cost** and readily available hospital compressed air lines.
- **Poor positioning accuracy** without closed-loop control due to air compressibility.
- **Noisy operation** and **energy inefficient** (Ōēł 10ŌĆō20% thermodynamic efficiency).

### 2.1.3 Applications in Medical Robotics
- **Pneumatic Muscle Actuators (PMA)** / McKibben muscles used in rehabilitation exoskeletons and orthoses.
- **Hand rehabilitation devices** (e.g., soft robotic gloves for stroke patients).
- **Pneumatically driven surgical instrument graspers** in laparoscopic systems.
- **MRI-compatible robots** ŌĆö non-magnetic pneumatic actuators are preferred inside strong magnetic fields where electric motors would interfere.
- **Active catheter steering** in minimally invasive cardiac procedures.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Buzzword:** *Pneumatic Artificial Muscle (PAM)* ŌĆö also called a *McKibben actuator* after its inventor Joseph L. McKibben (1950s). It produces a contractile force when internal air pressure increases.

### 2.1.4 Governing Equations

The force generated by a single-acting pneumatic cylinder is governed by the fundamental pressure-force relationship:

$$F_p \;=\; P_a \cdot A_e \;-\; P_{atm} \cdot A_r \;-\; F_{fric} \;-\; F_{load}$$

where the variables and units are rigorously defined in the formula sheet below.

---

## 2.2 Hydraulic Drives

### 2.2.1 Operating Principle
A hydraulic drive uses **incompressible fluid** (typically medical-grade mineral oil or water-glycol mixture) pressurised by a pump to pressures of **70 bar to 350 bar**. The pressurised fluid acts on a piston to produce linear motion, or on a vane/motor to produce rotary motion.

### 2.2.2 Salient Features
- **Highest power density** of all drive types ŌĆö capable of generating **up to 10 kW from a 1 kg actuator**.
- **Incompressible fluid** ŌĆö provides excellent stiffness, positioning accuracy, and load-bearing capacity.
- **Self-lubricating** ŌĆö hydraulic fluid provides continuous lubrication of moving parts.
- **Smooth and quiet** operation due to damping characteristics of the fluid.
- **Leakage hazard** ŌĆö fluid spills in an operating theatre are unacceptable; hence limited use inside the sterile field.
- **Bulky power pack** ŌĆö pump, reservoir, and filtration unit cannot be placed on the moving arm.
- **Risk of contamination** ŌĆö requires sealed circuits and biocompatible fluids.

### 2.2.3 Applications in Medical Robotics
- **Heavy-payload rehabilitation robots** (e.g., Lokomat ŌĆö gait trainer that supports the patient's body weight).
- **Patient-lifting and transfer robots** in hospitals.
- **Hydraulic surgical tables** and **operating table positioners**.
- **Force-feedback haptic devices** where high force output is required.
- **Prosthetic limb actuators** for above-knee amputees (e.g., Ossur Rheo Knee uses hydraulic damping).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** *Hydraulic drives are chosen when force output exceeds ~100 N and the system is not required to enter the patient's sterile body cavity.*

### 2.2.4 Governing Equations

$$F_h \;=\; P_h \cdot A_p \cdot \eta_m$$

The flow rate and power relations are stated in the master formula sheet that follows.

---

## 2.3 Mechanical Drives

### 2.3.1 Operating Principle
Mechanical drives transmit motion and power through **rigid or flexible solid elements** such as gears, racks, pinions, lead screws, ball screws, cams, linkages, belts, chains, and cable-and-pulley systems. They do not generate power; they only *redistribute* and *transform* the motion produced by a prime mover.

### 2.3.2 Salient Features
- **No energy conversion** ŌĆö therefore **100% mechanical efficiency** is theoretically achievable.
- **Deterministic motion** ŌĆö gear ratios provide exact, repeatable motion (no drift).
- **High stiffness** ŌĆö rigid links and gears provide excellent positional repeatability.
- **Backlash** is a primary limitation for precision robots.
- **Back-drivable** mechanical designs (cable drives, harmonic drives) are essential for safe human-robot interaction.
- **Wear and lubrication** requirements; potential generation of particulate matter (unacceptable in cleanrooms).

### 2.3.3 Applications in Medical Robotics
- **Harmonic drives** (strain-wave gears) ŌĆö used in almost every surgical robot (e.g., da Vinci Surgical System, Mako) for zero-backlash high-ratio reduction (**typical ratio 80:1 to 320:1**).
- **Cable-driven transmissions** (tendon drives) ŌĆö used in dexterous surgical manipulators and surgical snake robots.
- **Ball-screw and lead-screw actuators** in linear stages for imaging and biopsy positioning.
- **Cam-and-follower mechanisms** in endoscopic tip steering.
- **Rack-and-pinion** drives in patient-positioning couches.

### 2.3.4 Governing Equations

For a simple gear train, the velocity ratio is:

$$i \;=\; \dfrac{\omega_{in}}{\omega_{out}} \;=\; \dfrac{N_{out}}{N_{in}} \;=\; \dfrac{T_{out}}{T_{in}} \cdot \eta_g$$

where the variables and units are tabulated in the master formula sheet.

---

## 2.4 Electrical Drives (General Overview)

### 2.4.1 Operating Principle
Electrical drives use **electromagnetic energy conversion** through DC, AC, or special-purpose motors. They are the **most widely used drive type in modern medical robots** because of their precision, controllability, clean operation, and ease of integration with digital controllers.

### 2.4.2 Salient Features
- **High precision and controllability** ŌĆö easily interfaced with microcontrollers, FPGAs, and DSPs.
- **No fluid leaks** ŌĆö safe in sterile environments.
- **Compact and lightweight** ŌĆö power densities of **1 to 5 kW/kg** in modern servo motors.
- **Heat dissipation** must be managed in hermetically sealed enclosures.
- **EMI/EMC considerations** ŌĆö switching electronics can interfere with sensitive medical sensors (ECG, EEG).

### 2.4.3 Master Classification of Electrical Drives

The electrical drives required by the KTU 2024 syllabus are:
1. **DC Servo Motors**
2. **Stepper Motors**
3. **AC Servo Motors**

Each is analysed in detail in the following sub-sections.

---

## 2.5 DC Servo Motors

### 2.5.1 Operating Principle
A DC servo motor is a **separately excited or permanent-magnet DC motor** equipped with a position/speed feedback sensor (encoder, resolver, or tachogenerator). It operates in a **closed-loop configuration**, allowing precise control of position, velocity, and acceleration.

### 2.5.2 Salient Features
- **Excellent speed regulation** ŌĆö linear torque-speed characteristic.
- **Wide speed range** ŌĆö typically **0.1 RPM to 5000 RPM**.
- **High starting torque** ŌĆö suitable for accelerating inertial loads of robot joints.
- **Simple control law** ŌĆö armature voltage controls speed; armature current controls torque.
- **Brush wear** ŌĆö in brushed DC motors, brushes require periodic replacement (a disadvantage in sterile environments).
- **Brushless DC (BLDC) variants** eliminate this problem and dominate modern surgical robots.
- **High power-to-volume ratio** for given torque output.

### 2.5.3 Applications in Medical Robotics
- **Joint actuators in surgical robots** (e.g., da Vinci EndoWrist instruments use BLDC motors).
- **Haptic device motors** in tele-surgery master consoles.
- **Pump and valve actuators** in infusion and dialysis machines.
- **Endoscope tip orientation** motors in flexible endoscopes.
- **Rehabilitation exoskeleton joints** (e.g., ankle, knee).

### 2.5.4 Governing Equations

The electromagnetic torque generated is directly proportional to armature current:

$$T_{em} \;=\; K_t \cdot I_a$$

The back-EMF generated is directly proportional to angular velocity:

$$E_b \;=\; K_e \cdot \omega_m$$

The armature circuit follows Kirchhoff's voltage law:

$$V_a \;=\; E_b \;+\; I_a \cdot R_a \;+\; L_a \cdot \dfrac{dI_a}{dt}$$

---

## 2.6 Stepper Motors

### 2.6.1 Operating Principle
A stepper motor is a **brushless, synchronous electromagnetic device** that divides a full rotation into a discrete number of steps. Each electrical pulse advances the rotor by one **step angle** $\theta_s$. The motor can be operated **open-loop** because the discrete step nature provides inherent positional information.

### 2.6.2 Salient Features
- **Open-loop position control** ŌĆö no encoder needed in many applications.
- **Discrete step angles** ŌĆö common values are **1.8┬░ (200 steps/rev)**, **0.9┬░ (400 steps/rev)**, and **7.5┬░ (48 steps/rev)**.
- **Excellent holding torque at standstill** ŌĆö provides inherent braking.
- **Direct digital compatibility** ŌĆö driven directly by microcontroller pulse trains.
- **Resonance and missed-step problems** at high speeds or under inertial overloads.
- **Lower efficiency and higher heat generation** than servo motors at the same load.
- **Limited high-speed torque** ŌĆö torque drops sharply above 1000 RPM.
- **Microstepping** techniques can subdivide the step angle to **1/32 or 1/256** of a full step for smoother motion.

### 2.6.3 Applications in Medical Robotics
- **Sample-handling stages** in automated clinical laboratories and blood analysers.
- **Dispensing and pipetting robots** in pharmaceutical automation.
- **Positioning of imaging detectors** in CT, MRI, and X-ray tables (non-magnetic variants only).
- **Valve and damper actuators** in ventilators and anaesthesia machines.
- **Open-loop joint drives** in low-cost educational and rehabilitation robots.
- **Biopsy needle positioning stages** where high holding torque is required.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trap:** *Stepper motors are NOT typically used in the manipulator joints of a master-slave tele-surgical robot because the master console requires continuous smooth torque feedback. DC or AC servo motors are used instead.*

### 2.6.4 Governing Equations

The fundamental relationship for a stepper motor's step angle is:

$$\theta_s \;=\; \dfrac{360┬░}{N_s}$$

where the variables and units are tabulated in the master formula sheet that follows.

---

## 2.7 AC Servo Motors

### 2.7.1 Operating Principle
An AC servo motor is a **brushless synchronous or induction motor** driven by a variable-frequency, variable-amplitude inverter (VFD/VSD). The rotor position is sensed by a high-resolution encoder or resolver, enabling closed-loop vector control or direct torque control (DTC). Modern AC servos can also be **synchronous reluctance** or **PMSM** (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor) types.

### 2.7.2 Salient Features
- **Maintenance-free** ŌĆö no brushes, no commutator, sealed construction.
- **High power density** ŌĆö up to **5 kW/kg** in premium models.
- **Excellent dynamic response** ŌĆö bandwidth up to **1 kHz** in current loop.
- **High efficiency** ŌĆö typically **90ŌĆō95%** at rated load.
- **Precise field-oriented control (FOC)** ŌĆö decouples torque and flux for smooth motion.
- **Higher cost and complexity** of drive electronics compared to DC servos.
- **Sensitive to encoder failure** ŌĆö most failures are encoder-related, not motor-related.
- **Operates cleanly in sterile environments** ŌĆö sealed, low-particulate design.

### 2.7.3 Applications in Medical Robotics
- **Heavy-payload surgical robots** (e.g., Mako Rio, CyberKnife).
- **Linear accelerator patient-positioning robots** in radiation oncology.
- **Imaging robot gantries** in CT and MRI suites.
- **High-speed centrifuge drives** in laboratory automation.
- **Centrifuge and analysis robots** for high-throughput screening.
- **Haptic master consoles** requiring smooth torque feedback.

### 2.7.4 Governing Equations

The synchronous speed of an AC servo is governed by the supply frequency and number of pole pairs:

$$N_s \;=\; \dfrac{120 \cdot f}{P}$$

The slip in an induction-type AC servo is defined as:

$$s \;=\; \dfrac{N_s \;-\; N_r}{N_s}$$

where the variables and units are tabulated in the master formula sheet that follows.

# SECTION 3 ŌĆö STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö DC Servo Motor Steady-State Speed Equation

### 3.1.1 Statement of the Governing Equations

Consider a separately excited DC servo motor driving an inertial load. In the **steady-state condition** (i.e., when $\frac{dI_a}{dt} = 0$ and all transients have decayed), the two fundamental equations are the electromagnetic torque equation and the armature circuit KVL.

The electromagnetic torque developed by the motor is given by the following relation, which states that the torque is directly proportional to the armature current.

$$T_{em} = K_t \cdot I_a$$

The back-EMF induced in the armature conductors as the rotor rotates in the magnetic field is given by Faraday's law, which states that the back-EMF is directly proportional to the angular velocity of the rotor.

$$E_b = K_e \cdot \omega_m$$

Kirchhoff's voltage law applied to the armature circuit in the steady state (when the inductor behaves as a short circuit to DC, meaning $L_a \cdot \frac{dI_a}{dt} = 0$) yields the following algebraic balance equation.

$$V_a = I_a \cdot R_a + E_b$$

### 3.1.2 Derivation of Steady-State Speed

We start with the steady-state armature voltage equation, which is the KVL expression evaluated at $\frac{dI_a}{dt} = 0$.

$$V_a = I_a \cdot R_a + E_b$$

We then substitute the expression for the back-EMF, using the relation $E_b = K_e \cdot \omega_m$, into the KVL equation to obtain the intermediate expression that contains the unknown speed $\omega_m$.

$$V_a = I_a \cdot R_a + K_e \cdot \omega_m$$

We now isolate the term containing the speed $\omega_m$ on one side of the equation by moving the resistive voltage drop $I_a \cdot R_a$ to the left-hand side of the equation.

$$V_a - I_a \cdot R_a = K_e \cdot \omega_m$$

We finally divide both sides of the equation by the back-EMF constant $K_e$, assuming $K_e \neq 0$, to obtain the explicit expression for the steady-state angular velocity of the DC servo motor.

$$\omega_m = \dfrac{V_a - I_a \cdot R_a}{K_e}$$

This is the **KTU board-standard form** of the DC servo speed equation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Tip:** The corresponding **no-load speed** (when $I_a = 0$) is $\omega_0 = \frac{V_a}{K_e}$, and the **stall torque** (when $\omega_m = 0$) is $T_{stall} = \frac{K_t \cdot V_a}{R_a}$.

### 3.1.3 Numerical Example ŌĆö Worked Solution for KTU Practice

> **Problem:** A DC servo motor has $R_a = 0.5\ \Omega$, $K_e = 0.05\ \text{V┬Ęs/rad}$, and is supplied with $V_a = 24\ \text{V}$. The motor drives a load such that the steady-state armature current is $I_a = 2\ \text{A}$. Calculate (i) the back-EMF, (ii) the steady-state speed in rad/s and RPM, and (iii) the electromagnetic torque (assuming $K_t = K_e$ in SI units).

**Solution ŌĆö Step (i): Back-EMF Calculation**

We apply KVL to the armature circuit in steady state, $V_a = I_a R_a + E_b$, and substitute the given numerical values $V_a = 24$, $I_a = 2$, $R_a = 0.5$.

$$E_b = V_a - I_a \cdot R_a$$

$$E_b = 24 - (2)(0.5)$$

$$E_b = 24 - 1.0 = 23.0\ \text{V}$$

**Solution ŌĆö Step (ii): Angular Speed Calculation**

We use the steady-state speed equation $\omega_m = \frac{E_b}{K_e}$ with $E_b = 23.0$ and $K_e = 0.05$.

$$\omega_m = \dfrac{23.0}{0.05} = 460\ \text{rad/s}$$

Converting to revolutions per minute using the identity $1\ \text{rad/s} = \frac{60}{2\pi}\ \text{RPM}$:

$$N_m = \omega_m \cdot \dfrac{60}{2\pi}$$

$$N_m = 460 \cdot \dfrac{60}{2\pi} = \dfrac{27600}{6.2832} \approx 4393\ \text{RPM}$$

**Solution ŌĆö Step (iii): Electromagnetic Torque Calculation**

Using the SI-units identity $K_t = K_e$ (in N┬Ęm/A when $K_e$ is in V┬Ęs/rad) and the torque-current relation $T_{em} = K_t \cdot I_a$:

$$T_{em} = 0.05 \cdot 2 = 0.10\ \text{N┬Ęm}$$

**Result Summary:** $E_b = 23\ \text{V}$, $\omega_m = 460\ \text{rad/s} \approx 4393\ \text{RPM}$, $T_{em} = 0.10\ \text{N┬Ęm}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Stepper Motor Resolution and Pulse Frequency

### 3.2.1 Statement

A hybrid stepper motor has $N_s$ steps per revolution. It is driven by a pulse train of frequency $f_p$ pulses per second. Determine (a) the step angle in degrees, and (b) the relationship between $f_p$ and the resulting shaft speed in RPM.

### 3.2.2 Derivation of Step Angle

We start from the definition of step angle as the angular displacement produced by one electrical pulse, with a full mechanical revolution equal to $360┬░$ being divided into $N_s$ equal steps.

$$\theta_s = \dfrac{360┬░}{N_s}$$

For a typical hybrid stepper, $N_s = 200$ (i.e., 200 steps per revolution), and therefore the step angle evaluates to the following numerical value.

$$\theta_s = \dfrac{360┬░}{200} = 1.8┬░\ \text{per step}$$

This is the most common stepper resolution in industrial and medical automation.

### 3.2.3 Derivation of Shaft Speed

If the pulse train advances the motor at $f_p$ pulses per second, the number of steps executed in one second is numerically equal to $f_p$. Since each step produces an angular displacement of $\theta_s$, the angular velocity of the shaft in degrees per second is $f_p \cdot \theta_s$.

$$\omega_{deg} = f_p \cdot \theta_s = f_p \cdot \dfrac{360┬░}{N_s}$$

We convert this angular velocity to revolutions per minute. First, we convert degrees per second to revolutions per second by dividing by $360┬░$, and then convert revolutions per second to RPM by multiplying by 60.

$$N_{rpm} = \dfrac{f_p \cdot \theta_s}{360┬░} \cdot 60$$

We substitute $\theta_s = \frac{360┬░}{N_s}$ and simplify the resulting expression by cancelling the $360┬░$ factor in the numerator and denominator.

$$N_{rpm} = \dfrac{f_p \cdot \frac{360┬░}{N_s}}{360┬░} \cdot 60$$

$$N_{rpm} = \dfrac{f_p \cdot 60}{N_s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Final Form:** $\boxed{N_{rpm} = \dfrac{60 \cdot f_p}{N_s}}$

### 3.2.4 Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A stepper motor with $N_s = 200$ steps/rev is driven at $f_p = 1000\ \text{Hz}$. Calculate (i) the step angle, and (ii) the shaft speed in RPM.

**Solution (i):**

$$\theta_s = \dfrac{360┬░}{200} = 1.8┬░$$

**Solution (ii):**

$$N_{rpm} = \dfrac{60 \cdot 1000}{200} = \dfrac{60000}{200} = 300\ \text{RPM}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö AC Servo Synchronous Speed and Slip

### 3.3.1 Statement

An AC servo induction motor has $P = 4$ poles and is supplied from a $f = 50\ \text{Hz}$ three-phase source. The motor runs at an actual speed of $N_r = 1425\ \text{RPM}$. Calculate (i) the synchronous speed, (ii) the slip, and (iii) the frequency of the rotor current.

### 3.3.2 Solution (i): Synchronous Speed

We apply the synchronous speed formula $N_s = \frac{120 f}{P}$ with $f = 50$ and $P = 4$.

$$N_s = \dfrac{120 \cdot 50}{4} = \dfrac{6000}{4} = 1500\ \text{RPM}$$

### 3.3.3 Solution (ii): Slip

We apply the slip definition $s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ with $N_s = 1500$ and $N_r = 1425$.

$$s = \dfrac{1500 - 1425}{1500} = \dfrac{75}{1500} = 0.05 = 5\%$$

### 3.3.4 Solution (iii): Rotor Current Frequency

The frequency of the induced current in the rotor bars is given by $f_r = s \cdot f$, which evaluates to the following numerical value.

$$f_r = 0.05 \cdot 50 = 2.5\ \text{Hz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** At standstill ($N_r = 0$), $s = 1$ and $f_r = f$, which is why an induction motor draws a high inrush current at start. In medical robots, soft-start drives are used to avoid this surge.

## 4.5 Harmonic Drive Block Diagram (Most Common Mechanical Drive in Surgical Robots)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[SERVO MOTOR INPUT SHAFT] --> WG[WAVEGENERATOR ELLIPTICAL BEARING]:::core
    WG --> FS[FLEXSPLINE THIN CUP TOOTHED]:::core
    FS --> CS[CIRCULAR SPLINE RIGID RING GEAR]:::core
    CS --> OUT[OUTPUT SHAFT SLOW ROTATION]:::out

    WG -. High speed rotation .-> WG2[Rotates slightly faster than FS]
    FS -. Deforms elliptically .-> FS2[Engages two teeth fewer than CS]
    CS -. Fixed tooth count .-> CS2[Typically two more teeth than FS]

    classDef core fill:#f4b084,stroke:#c55a11,color:#000000
    classDef out fill:#70ad47,stroke:#385723,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A harmonic drive achieves a reduction ratio equal to the difference in tooth counts divided by the flexspline tooth count. If the flexspline has 200 teeth and the circular spline has 202 teeth, the reduction ratio is **202 / 2 = 100:1**. This is why harmonic drives are the **gold standard** in surgical robot wrists.
